cards.front: What does a filter do in frequency domain terms?
cards.back: Makes |H(ω)| = 0 for unwanted frequencies, allowing others to pass at |H(ω)| = 1.
|H(ω)| = 1 for |ω| ≤ B, 0 for |ω| > B. B = bandwidth. Rectangular shape.
|H(ω)| = 0 for |ω| < B, 1 for |ω| ≥ B. Blocks low frequencies, passes high.
|H(ω)| = 1 for B₁ ≤ |ω| ≤ B₂, 0 elsewhere. Passes a frequency band, needs two cutoffs.
|H(ω)| = 0 for B₁ ≤ |ω| ≤ B₂, 1 elsewhere. Blocks a band, passes the rest. Also called notch filter.
For real-valued h(t): H(−ω) = H*(ω) → |H(−ω)| = |H(ω)|. All real systems have even magnitude.

cards.front: How does cascading affect frequency responses?
cards.back: H_total(ω) = H₁(ω) · H₂(ω). Magnitudes multiply. If either is 0 at some ω, cascade is 0 there.

cards.front: Cascade LPF (cutoff B₁) + HPF (cutoff B₂ < B₁) → ?
cards.back: Band-pass filter with passband from B₂ to B₁. HPF kills below B₂, LPF kills above B₁.

cards.front: How many parameters define LPF vs BPF?
cards.back: LPF: 1 parameter (B). BPF: 2 parameters (B₁, B₂). HPF: 1 parameter. BSF: 2 parameters.

cards.front: Sinusoidal input at ω₀ through ideal LPF with cutoff B. Output if ω₀ > B?
cards.back: Zero. |H(ω₀)| = 0 since ω₀ is in the stopband. Output y(t) = A·0·cos(...) = 0.
Another name for band-stop filter (BSF). Removes a narrow band of frequencies — e.g., 50/60 Hz power-line noise from ECG.
Perfectly rectangular magnitude: |H| is exactly 1 in passband, exactly 0 in stopband. No transition region. Physically unrealisable.
